name: timers
context: Create and enable systemd timer units for calendar-based or monotonic scheduled work
info: This skill covers `.timer` unit authoring and activation. It explains how timers pair with services, the difference between wall-clock schedules such as `OnCalendar=` and monotonic schedules such as `OnBootSec=` or `OnUnitActiveSec=`, and the operational switches agents actually need: `Persistent=` for catching up missed runs, `RandomizedDelaySec=` for spreading load, `AccuracySec=` for coalescing wakeups, and `WakeSystem=` for suspend-aware scheduling. Agents use this skill to replace cron with a first-class systemd workflow that shares activation state, logs, and dependency handling with the rest of the manager.
body:
# Timer Units

A `.timer` unit schedules activation of another unit, usually a `.service` with the same base name. Timers are better than ad hoc cron jobs when you want scheduling, activation state, dependency management, and logs to stay in the same toolchain.

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Nightly backup timer

[Timer]
Unit=backup.service
OnCalendar=*-*-* 03:00:00
Persistent=true
RandomizedDelaySec=15m

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
```

Validate calendar expressions before installation:

```bash
systemd-analyze calendar '*-*-* 03:00:00'
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now backup.timer
systemctl list-timers --all
```

Remember that the timer activates the service. If the service is already active when the timer elapses, systemd does not spawn a second instance. For repeated scheduled work, design the paired service so it can run, finish, and become inactive again. See [[systemd/troubleshooting#timer-not-firing]] when the schedule appears correct but the service never runs.

sections.title: Pairing timers with services
sections.body: A timer normally activates a service with the same base name, such as `backup.timer` -> `backup.service`. You can override that with `Unit=`. Timers do not execute shell commands directly; the activated unit defines the work. This is why [[systemd/services]] is the natural prerequisite for timer authoring.

sections.title: Calendar and monotonic triggers
sections.body: Use `OnCalendar=` for wall-clock schedules and use monotonic settings such as `OnBootSec=`, `OnStartupSec=`, `OnUnitActiveSec=`, or `OnUnitInactiveSec=` for delays relative to boot or previous activity. Mix them only when the semantics are truly intentional. Use [[systemd/analyze#calendar]] to normalize and preview calendar expressions.

sections.title: Persistence and jitter
sections.body: `Persistent=true` records the last elapsed wall-clock run and catches up when the machine was powered off during the scheduled time. `RandomizedDelaySec=` spreads otherwise synchronized runs across an interval. `AccuracySec=` controls how tightly systemd should try to fire relative to the requested time and defaults toward power-saving coalescing.

sections.title: Inspection and state
sections.body: Use `systemctl list-timers --all` to see next and last elapse times, and `systemctl show TIMER -p NextElapseUSecRealtime --value` for parsable state. When a timer seems healthy but the job did not complete, inspect the paired service with [[systemd/systemctl]] and its logs with [[systemd/journalctl]].

links.context: Create the paired service unit that the timer activates
Validate and normalize calendar expressions before installing the timer
Enable and start the timer after installation
Inspect the activated service logs after the timer fires
Debug timers that never elapse or never trigger useful work

related.note: Timers almost always activate services rather than raw commands
Use systemd-analyze calendar to validate OnCalendar expressions
systemctl is used to enable, start, and inspect timers

scripts.name: Install Timer
scripts.description: Render a `.timer` unit, verify it, install it into the correct scope, reload the manager, and optionally enable or start it

SYSTEMD_USE_SUDO.description: Use sudo for system-level writes and manager calls

SYSTEMD_TIMER_NAME.description: Timer unit name, with or without the .timer suffix

SYSTEMD_TIMER_UNIT.description: Unit that should be activated when the timer elapses

SYSTEMD_TIMER_ON_UNIT_ACTIVE_SEC.description: Delay relative to last activation of the target unit

SYSTEMD_TIMER_ON_UNIT_INACTIVE_SEC.description: Delay relative to last deactivation of the target unit

name.description: Timer name, with or without .timer suffix

unit.description: Unit to activate when the timer elapses

depends.reason: Timers usually activate a service unit
Reloading, enabling, starting, and inspecting the timer happens through systemctl
Calendar expressions and timer files should be validated before activation
